speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 2nd of July, 1995. Survivor is Peter Hersch, last name at birth Pinchas Herskovics. Interviewer Ruth Osborne, Sydney, Australia. The language is English, and this is Tape one. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 2, 1995. I'm Ruth Osborne, that's O-S-B-O-R-N-E. I'm in Sydney, Australia, and I'm doing an interview with Peter Hersch. OK. Peter, would you please tell us your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Peter Hersch, H-E-R-S-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your-- was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, my name was Pinchas Herskovics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-R-S-K-O-V-I-C-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. And what was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday was the 2nd of July, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, same as today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm today 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was born was Czechoslovakia, and the place was called Loza, which is Loza next to Irshava, which was quite a big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Loza? How do you spell Loza?

speaker: interviewee
text: Loza-- L-O-Z-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. OK. Well, to begin with, would you like to tell us something about your family and where you lived, and what life was like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I was a young, young boy, life was very beautiful for us. We were very, very-- it's a religious little town, and most of the Jews, the whole life revolved around the synagogue. So that means in the morning, early in the morning, when we got up about 4 o'clock or 5 o'clock in the morning, we went to cheder. And from cheder, we came home, had breakfast. Then we went to the normal school, which was with everybody, like non-Jews as well as Jews. It was a very, very nice little community. And after we came back from school, we went back to cheder till late in the evening. So that was life when I was a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go to the synagogue as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning and the afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went every morning and every afternoon. And not only-- you know, the whole town, as I said, revolved that around that sort of life. It was a real shtetl. It was a very beautiful, enjoyable life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big of a town was Loza?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you the population, but we were sort of, as I said, Loza-Irshava was more or less together. There was a huge Jewish community there. And every--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody seemed to know one another. And the feeling, the love and affection for one another was very great.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were a religious community, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of things did they do for living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had-- some of them had stores, like shops. Others did commerce. And also there were lots of rural area and lots of fields, you know, growing all sorts of things-- wheat, corn, things like that. It was not just the one thing. The earth was very sort of good earth, and people were growing things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were any of the Jews involved in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes. They were involved in every sphere of life. Doesn't matter what you touched, on from chemists to doctors to lawyers to, to farmers and businessmen. It wasn't just a one thing. And we lived very, very harmoniously with our neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jews and Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which were non-Jews. The greatest of respect we had for one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't remember there being anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was no anti-Semitism that I can remember until the Hungarians came in and the Germans came in. That's when it started. Then, of course, you had a lot of people join the party-- I'm talking about the non-Jewish people. And that's when it started to be quite anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us, what were-- was your father's name, and what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Ephraim Herskovics, of course, which we called him Phraim-- everybody called him. And my mother's name was Rachelle. And we were six children. So we were three girls and three boys. And of course, we lived with my grandparents. We stayed in their house. My father was one of the sons among six girls or so. I think it was five or six sisters he had. So of course, he was the spoiled one, so I was told. And my grandparents, of course, spoiled us children. And he was in business. He was dealing in things. You know, we had quite a normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did he deal in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from what I remember, it was buying things and selling it. He used to be away on the road quite a lot. But I never took much notice of what it was, as long as everything was going all right. And we also had big, big areas of land behind our house, farms-- not farms, but land, growing things, which people were working, you know, on the land. I remember that very clearly. I even had a horse there. So I should have. So that was life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a good life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Good life, a good life it was. And Yom Tov, of course, on the holidays, everybody-- as I said, it revolved around the holidays, whether it was Sukkot or Pesach or Purim. In fact, I remember Purim was a very interesting holiday because the non-Jewish teachers used to also dress up as, you know, in Purim things and come to our house. And it was a very beautiful life, as far as, you know, the family life was concerned. But of course, it didn't last very long that I can remember. It changed, and as I said, when they came in. But I want to tell you one incident which I-- which is very, very much in my mind, and which I remember very clearly. When the Hungarians and the Germans came in, all of a sudden the Jewish children were separated from the non-Jewish children. Up till then, we were all going together to school. And this particular teacher, she was a-- she joined the party, obviously, but not her husband. And when we-- from then on, all the kids, that means whether they were 5 years, 6 years, or 15 or 16, we were all together in school. And she was very-- I would say, very anti-Semitic. And she used to take-- pick on the smaller ones and hit them, and just for the sake of hitting them, and calling the different names.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember sitting back-- can you imagine? I was not quite 13, but I was already with the big boys, sitting back. And I waited till came to my brother Stern, which his name was Herschlag, after my two grandparents' name. And when she started to hit him, I jumped up from where I was and I took the stick. I broke it to pieces and I threw it at her, and I took him and sent him home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can imagine. The whole town was shaking because for somebody to do something like that, it's unheard of, especially under those conditions. But luckily that her husband, he came to my parents. And I wouldn't go back to school. And I said, I'm not going back to school. So I was a rebel in those days too, you see. I wouldn't go back to school till her husband came, and he begged my parents to let me go back and to make me go back to school because there could be repercussions. If she would have reported that, really, to the authorities, could have been in big trouble. So eventually I went back to school. But that was something which I haven't forgotten. And when I survived the war-- you can imagine it stayed with me-- I went back to that school to visit and tell her that I haven't forgotten it.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was still there, because he was I think the headmaster by that time. But I didn't do anything about it. Because I still certainly could have told the Russians, because the Russians occupied that area. See, that was Czechoslovakia. Then it was the Hungarians occupied it. Then Russian-- Russia occupied it. And then, well, it became USSR. It became Zakarpatska. Ukraine, now it's called. And today it's still Ukraine. So that place changed hands quite a bit. So that was one little incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when? Just before the war, more or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's all just-- oh yeah, it was before, before the war. But then we were segregated already. The Jews were already segregated at school. And then there's other little stories about rabbis, you know. We, we had certain rabbis that were teaching us, and-- and we-- if we were belted by them, we sort of took revenge in the end of the term. So that sort of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what was the story? Tell me that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the story was we had a synagogue which was right on a hill, and there were lovely trees there. And the rabbi was teaching us. And you used to-- the whole week you were-- you were studying, and then Thursday was the time when he, he started to, to ask questions, you know? There was more or less the exam every Thursday, and if you didn't know it, God help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you ask questions during the week, you know, he used to sometimes fall asleep. And you couldn't-- you see, or you'll wake him, and you say, can I ask you that questions? And he sort of mumbled. So one day-- we, we were naughty-- we decided to fix him, because he was very-- Thursday night, we used to get beltings from him. You know, if you didn't know your part, God help you. So we bought some glue, and as he was sitting and we asked questions-- he wouldn't answer us-- we glued his beard to the table. And that poor man, suddenly he started to lift his head to answer a question, and then the beard was stuck to the table. So of course we get-- we got plenty. Trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just little, little things that happened. And then, of course, Yom Tov was very special to us. Very special, because on Pesach for instance, I used to visit my grandparents in Munkacs in the [NON-ENGLISH] Mukacevo, which was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that very far away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say about an hour and a half or so on the bus from where we lived. And Pesach, I remember, my grandmother-- because once a year, you didn't go often there. Or two hours in a bus--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's mother used to stand in the kitchen and make latkes. That was from morning till night. You can imagine all the grandchildren, not just from us but from my mother's sisters that lived near. They were all there, and there was-- the house was packed. I remember those incidents, you know, those things. And at home, of course, we had-- our grandparents lived with us, or we lived with them, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father's sisters? Did they live with you too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't live with us. They lived in different places, and for instance, one was living-- Malke-- next-- not far from us, a place called Bilky. And I used to love to go to her. She was one of my favorite aunts. And all of us-- and my father and she were very, very close. Whenever, you see, they used to come-- all the sisters used to come for K'vurahs, which means to the cemetery, which was in our place. And of course, she used to come more often because she didn't live that far. And I could walk to her place. In an hour, I could be there. You see, it was very close, one place next to another. And her-- she had one son, and she lived with her son. And he was the kosher butcher in Bilky, so everybody knew him. And his name was also Ephraim. So very close-knit, we were very close. And we had lots of other cousins in Irshava, which was next door to us. So we used to go to each other for Yom Tov. Yom Tov was a beautiful occasion. Everybody was visiting everybody. Pesach was actually special. So that was life in that little-- in the little town. And of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there only the one synagogue in the town? Was it small?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a big synagogue, yes. And it was a beis medrash. There was a-- you know, another one for overflow for davening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the rabbi's name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that the rabbi's name. I can still remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the beard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I can still-- There were many rabbis. They used to teach us in different classes in cheder. But this one, I-- I remember his face, but I can't remember his name. Shouldn't have done that, should we? But we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, that was one little thing. And then I remember another incident at school. See, as we went to the cheder, some of the boys used to throw-- not stones. But winter, especially, was very cold, and also we had big snow, snow falling there. See, it's in the Carpathian Mountains, so you can imagine. And they used to throw snow at us. You know, those things. So the next day when I went to school, I found out who it was. I took him, just put his face in the snow. So you know, I didn't let them get away with it. But it was all in fun. There was no animosity between us and the non-Jewish people. And I said, it only started towards the end. And then from there, as I said to you, my brother, one of my-- my eldest brother, he had an operation. And he wasn't well, and the wounds didn't even heal, and he was sent home from the hospital, from Munkacs. It's one-- one thing that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I think it was on the stomach. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Appendix?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appendix, or burst-- something with a burst. It must have been either appendix, or whatever. And they sent him home. And you can imagine, soon after that they took us to the ghetto, you know, to Munkacs. And he was sick in bed. And now that's something which upset me very much when I think about it. So we had to take him outside when the Germans came and the Hungarian, those, those-- the soldiers with them. And we had to leave him in the-- on the-- on the ground, like in the front, in front of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: The garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: The garden, yes. And we were all taken to the ghetto. First of all, we were taken to a place in Irshava, which was next to us, in a place-- it was like a school-- for a few days. And we didn't know what happened to him. They told us they're going to take him to hospital, of course. We never-- we tried to find out, and we couldn't find out anything what happened to him. But he was left there while we all went together. And they told us we could only have a few kilos with us, you know, whatever clothes. So we all dressed, I think, in lots and lots of clothes, all of us, because we were told we're going to a farm somewhere, who knows where. Anyway, we ended up-- from there we were taken to Mukacevo in ghetto, which is a ghetto, brick ghetto. They made bricks there. And people from all over the areas were taken there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we were in the ghetto. The whole families, each family, got a little place-- not a house, or anything, it was all open. And we were sort of lying on the ground. You can imagine how my parents would have felt. And we were hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the aunties that lived outside the ghetto-- they were not taken yet-- she used to come and bring us some food. That was my mother's sister, one of the sisters that lived in Munkacs itself. She used to bring us food into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they-- when they came and took you away, just before that, can we go back with you to what were the first sort of stirrings in the town? Sort of the first time you felt frightened about what was going happen to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, glad you did mention that, because I just skipped that as I was going on. We were told that we were going to be taken away. So of course, the whole town congregated at the synagogue and praying. And that's where I was supposed to be bar mitzvah just at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't have the bar mitzvah, because everybody was getting aliyahs, being called up, and had [NON-ENGLISH]. And everybody was sort of-- it was like-- I remember the words they used, like Tisha B'av, which means when the Temple was destroyed. You know, that was the biggest disaster, tragedy for the Jewish people. And they all more or less felt that's exactly like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a very, very terrible time in town. And everybody was getting things ready and packing. We didn't know what was happening. I mean, us children, they didn't tell us much, but I remember how the elders of the community, they got together, and there was just panic in town. I mean, we didn't know what was happening. And they-- you can imagine, everybody had to leave their houses. And we knew it was happening all around us. So our turn, when it was coming within a few days, they-- I think we were told when it was coming. And it was a terrible--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody when they could, try to escape? I mean, did anybody leave the town that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I knew of. I mean, I didn't-- I'm sure some of them tried. But I certainly-- our family didn't because we were all small. I was 13, my oldest sister-- she was a couple of years older than me, Helen-- and the rest was all smaller. So you had from little to, to to the-- Helen was 14 or 15, and my brother was 16 or 17, you see, so-- and the rest was all small. We were six of us, so you can imagine, they were all very tiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ella, Ella was the youngest. She would have been-- she would have been about six, something like that because she didn't even start at school yet. So she was the smallest. Then it was-- Sarah was next, then Hersch Leib, my other brother. Then there was me, and then there was Helen, and then there was Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were, as you can imagine, a lot of children. They, they certainly-- most families had six or eight children. So what sort of standard of living did they have? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't a luxurious standard of-- It was just a normal standard of living. We had food and clothes. As far as I know, there was no luxuries. And there's no such thing as going for holidays. The holidays was going to visit our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- but it was a comfortable life, and it was a meaningful life. You see, the richness was when it came a Friday night. You can't measure it in anything except the love and affection and the warmth of the place, in a subtle way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody dressed up in their best clothes and then we went for sort of for walks, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we lived near a river, so people used to go to the river and swim in summer and sort of fish. I think the normal things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play sport at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't play sport. I didn't have time for sport. We were, as I said, busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the synagogue most of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. As I said, it was a very, very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Full.

speaker: interviewee
text: --full life. And when you have time, as I said, there was on a Saturday. Saturday there was no work. And we were very observant, so you started off Saturday morning to the synagogue. You came home for lunch. Then in the afternoon you went back to the synagogue. So it was really full time with the, the religious observance. But it was lovely. The feeling was not that you have to go, you were happy to go because everybody else was there. So you knew everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd be the odd man out if you didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you didn't, yes. So that's what life revolved around our little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you not remember your parents discussing the possibility of what was going to happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, but they didn't realize. You see, we couldn't believe the horror that is waiting for us, because they didn't tell us about the death camps. They said we were going to go-- I remember this. They were discussing it. They were going to go somewhere and work on farms, and the family will be together. That's why we were-- we had to have warm clothes, to take warm clothes with us. And that was the whole thing. And of course, I remember also that all the silver and the cutlery and the crockery and all the good things, we gave to our neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: To look after.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, to look after, to put away. And if there was some gold, they, they, they were hiding it, because they didn't-- they knew that we mustn't take anything like that with us. So all the linen was at our neighbor's place, to the, you know, non-Jewish people. We gave to look after it for us. So that's what everybody did. Everybody in town passed on their belongings, all the good things, and the rest you left the house just standing the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a detail, but it's interesting in terms of what happened later. Did they give you receipts for those things, or was it just done on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no receipts or anything. And when I came home, actually-- well, I'll tell you about that after-- but they-- we knew, because we were all told if anybody that comes home, that so-and-so has got, you know, stuff for us. We knew which neighbor had received the goods. And they, they, they were looking after it for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they give it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a long story. I'll tell you about it, because I want to tell you when I came back in a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But as I said, the neighbors were very nice and very good. And we were friends with their children, with their families. It was nothing but the best of-- we used to go into their house visiting. They came to our house visiting, you know? So a very normal neighborly life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until the war, anti-Semitism wasn't an issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I can remember. No, it was not an issue in that part of the world. In fact, it was a very nice life. I remember that people used to, for instance, on Sabbath-- you know, the religious people, they can't carry things. So they used to put on their tallis, that's their shawl, their prayer shawls, and sort of go to the synagogue. And everybody was greeting you. It's very, very nice feeling between the peoples.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they notify you that you were going to be taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they must have told the elders of the town, the community. There's always--

speaker: interviewer
text: A council. Was there a Jewish council?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well there was for the Jewish affairs, like for the synagogue and for the anything to do with that. And they would have notified them. Because we were definitely told that we will be taken at a certain day, which was about a week or so they gave us. Or two days, I don't know. I know it was panic stations when it happened. And we all packed, and everybody, the whole town was packing up. And it was predominately a Jewish town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So most people, really--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town were moving out.

speaker: interviewee
text: --were moving out. So the non-Jewish people that were in town were looking after all the possessions, whatever anybody gave them. So that was life before the war that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think perhaps that's why they were-- they got along so well, because you were the majority? I mean, because the Jews in the town were the majority?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think that the respect for the-- was there between the two peoples, because I do remember very vividly, for instance, our next door neighbors and at school. Don't forget, I mean, we did mix. We didn't just go with Jewish people to school, we went with everybody. And it was a big-- you know, mixed, we went boys and girls were together. And there was nothing-- and the Jewish boys and girls mixed like they were brothers and sisters. You know, there was no-- I didn't feel it personally, and I'm sure others didn't. Mind you, we were-- only difference was that we had payos.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had payos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did too. And that was the only thing that was different. But can't remember them ever being anti-us. They might have been, but they didn't show it to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you wear yarmulkes all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Not actually yarmulke, but we had a hat. Listen, in that area of the world was so cold in any case, we had the hats.

speaker: interviewer
text: To give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the big hats, but just the same as they wear here. So that part was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So, so you were notified that you were going to be taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your parents being very disturbed, upset about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very, very upset, and very worried. Mainly for the children, you can imagine, all of us. How to feed, how to-- where we were going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sure my father, being a soldier-- you know, he was in the Czech army-- he would have known a lot more than he would have told us, because there was a whole group of the young men, you know, before, before they were taking us. They were soldiers in the army. They had to, to-- so they would have known much more, and they would have been aware of what was happening. And they would have read newspapers, too, what was happening, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we, the youngsters, we didn't know. We were told we were going to go to work, and we have to be-- And I remember my mother dressed me. I don't know how many shirts I had on, how many underwear, underpants I had on, and jackets and, you know, sweaters. I was looking like a barrel. I could hardly walk, but she said you'll need it. You'll need it because it'll get cold. And then we can-- you know, you can take off layers. And it has to be washed, and we don't know where we're going to go. And that was every one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole thing was concentrated on your-- on dressing you up warmly. Which was just as well, because we did need it. And of course-- So do you remember-- was it just your family went on that day, or were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the whole town, all of them. We all went together to the ghetto. The whole place, the whole town, we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was-- first of all, they took us to a place in Irshava. It was a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the bigger town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bigger. Yes, next door to us. I mean, you walked it in a half hour, just there it was. And we couldn't go back home anymore. I remember some of them tried to run and get something, or they forgot, you know. And they were warning us not to go, because they can-- they'll catch us, and you can be in trouble. They didn't say shoot, but you can be in big, big trouble. But some people went back and they said the township was like a ghost town. Nobody was there. It was eerie. And from there, the train-- there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the ghetto in Irshava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Irshava. Then they took us to the ghetto in Munkacs, in Mukacevo. And that's where we all went. The whole town went there. People not just from our town, but from all towns surrounding the area, in all the smaller towns, because Munkacs was a big, big city. And there's a huge Jewish community there. I think more than half the people in Mukacevo, I believe, were Jewish people. So they had this huge place, and very, very orthodox. One of the top of rabbis-- I don't know his name-- the whole world, even today, talks about him was in Munkacs. He was very, very, very well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to this big ghetto, and it was terrible, as you can imagine. The crying and the filth, and we were hungry. And we didn't know what was happening. And the nastiness from the people that were looking after it. Like the Hungarians were there, mainly, and there were very, very nasty beatings and things like that. And I remember that's-- sorry. I think we'll finish that tape.
